comments.title: Inquiry into Europes magic community
comments.content:
John Greer,

I am a student of the Dolmen Arch study course and the Druid Magic Handbook. I live in Europe.

This year I am between university studies and am taking the time to pursue personal research and writing in spiritual ecology. I want to know more about how mystery traditions and magical practices have influenced our ecological relationships and land management practices.

This Magic Monday I am asking for your recommendation of serious chat/blogging/study/practice groups in English for Druidism and/or other active Mystery schools. Or just in the Occult in general.

Up until now I have conducted my study and practice largely alone and would welcome the chance to connect to others in the European occult community. However, I am largely unaware of the current occult milieu in Europe or the UK and would value your advice before venturing forth.

May clarity shine on you,
Igor Blumer

replies.title: Re: Inquiry into Europes magic community
replies.content:
Hello,

Although I am not from Europe or the UK, I have read a few books of magic from authors living or practising magic in the UK and France. One author that might be of interest to you would be Josephine McCarthy : she as written quite a few books on magic one of them being Magic of the North Gate, Powers of the Land, the Stones and the Ancients. She states that the book is not a beginners book but 'it is a book of techniques and methods for workings magicians, priestesses, and occultists-techniques that take us deeper into magic and therefore deeper into ourselves.' The book has chapters on magic of the land and working with land powers.

What might also be of interest to you is the fact that her approach includes taking into account the importance of the Ancients in the European sense, while working with the mysteries, without being aligned to only one magical system. I am paraphrasing and attempting to summarize her work, and truly believe that consulting her web site and the web site of her school of magic would do her better justice.

Her school of magic can be accessed for free on the web at www.quareia.com . Both she and her school have a blog, and the school as a forum for beginning magicians. The Quareia Apprentice Study Guide is a very nice introduction to her approach to the mysteries as is the first chapters of her beginners course.

I personally have read a few books of our gracious host and of hers and find the similarities and differences quite fascinating and stimulating i.e. good fodder for meditation. I have not taken the plunge one way or another and plan to pursue my endeavours about the mysteries by reading books on druidism published by our host to complement my search for my calling. I am also prudent as I have taken note of the fact that both have warned that once one has started down a certain path one cannot truly abandon said path and 'forget about it' without one risking some 'inconveniences or blowback'.

Hope this helps,

Sapienter Si Sincere

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi, JMG- I'm hoping for some advice in getting over a mental block I'm having with divination. As a bit of background: completed the LRM coursework a little more than a year ago now, and have am slowly working my way through PoW/CoP, but I've let my daily tarot readings slide for the last several months, without feeling like I ever achieved much proficiency to speak of with the Tarot. When I was practicing regularly, I was doing the three-card draw outlined in LRM, with the question "what do I most need to understand about the upcoming day", with the first card representing self, the second situation, and the third outcome. I understand, intellectually at least, that the purpose of divination is as a kind of formalized way to let an intuitive sense of a situation speak for itself, without a censoring conscious mind or emotional investment getting in the way, and the idea of practicing daily reads on trivial things to get a certain amount of fluency before using an oracle to guide decisions of import makes a lot of sense to me. Where I found/find myself struggling is that when I read people asking questions about divination on this forum, they're usually framed as implicit "yes/no" questions, with a "positive" reading indicating that a certain action should be undertaken, and a "negative" reading indicating that it should not. I find myself pretty stuck in this as my default frame for divination as a whole, but I consistently run into the same kind of trouble when applying it to a daily reading: since I don't have the option to choose whether or not to live through the day to come, if I get, for example, a negative "outcome" card for the day ahead, I'm not sure what to do with it- as often as not, it might predict a certain kind of negative emotional flavor or generally trivial bad luck, but I have a hard time figuring out what good it does me to know in advance that I might have a comparitively lousy day, and I'm always a bit concerned about self-fulfilling prophecies. All of which decreases my motivation to continue with the practice. I'd imagine this is a fairly common situation, but it's still the one I'm stuck in at the moment- any suggestions as to how to approach this more constructively?
Thanks much, as always!
-NPM

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Ah, I see the problem. You're treating the reading as a statement of unchangeable fact rather than a discussion of possibilities and strategies. The next time you cast a three-card reading like that, look at it as advice concerning action rather than as something you just have to endure. If the outcome's an unwelcome card, say, read it as "if I do [card 1] in response to the situation [card 2], I'll get the unwelcome outcome [card 3] -- so I should do something other than [card 1]!" I've found that this is a more productive approach, and it very often allows me to avoid unwelcome outcomes.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you- that's very helpful, and also helps me with another puzzle I was having, which was about the function of the "self" card- considering it as the way I'll be "inclined" to act (in a "the stars incline, they do not compel" sense), with the outcome showing the results of that inclination, makes the whole structure of the spread fall into place in a way it never had for me before!

replies.title: Divination
replies.content:
Hi NPM, I read your post with interest, as I also am a student of the Tarot.
Regarding your question on yes/no answers and self-fulfilling prophecies, I have a few thoughts.
There is no need to surrender your agency to a Tarot reading: if you accept that statement, then yes/no questions are often inadvisable - rather you can frame the question more open ended as a way of seeking useful information, and you get to make decisions off that information. Of course you would take the information very seriously.

When a negative reading comes up, you could see it as a sign to be vigilant, and to steel yourself for an upcoming challenge.
The cards may be informing you of an upcoming event that is unavoidable - but you still have agency in how you prepare for and respond to the event. Or they may be warning you of a fork in the road ahead, and that one way has serious dangers, and is to be avoided.

Best wishes

replies.title: Re: A Strange Awakening
replies.content:
There are studies indicating that many yoga poses and sequences, as well as breathing practices, may have travelled in the opposite direction, from the west to India in the late 1800s and early 1900s. Those poses and sequences originated in gymnastic systems from Denmark, Sweden and France, and came to India with British colonial troops before becoming integrated with yoga. The story can be found in Mark Singletons "Yoga Body" among other sources.
- Mattias

replies.title: Re: A Strange Awakening
replies.content:
Hi Matthias,
Your right. Christopher Wallis did a U-tube video a few years back in which he talked about the origin of yoga. In the lecture he praised Mark Singletons book.

But in their turn, certain aspects of Indian physical culture influenced British physical culture. Training with light wooden Indian clubs became part of regular army PT in Britain and the US. It even became part of British swordsmanship.

- Spork -

replies.title: Re: A Strange Awakening
replies.content:
A bit of synchronicity with our posts here! I use Indian clubs a few times a week and really enjoy them. Quick tip for anyone considering taking them up: go for way less weight than you think you can handle. I was lifting some pretty heavy weights when I got my first pair and foolishly went for a 5 lb pair, thinking "5 lbs isn't heavy at all!"

Well, for Indian clubs, it's super heavy. 3 lbs was the standard weight when it was briefly an Olympic sport and is a pretty good weight for general exercise. A 1 lb pair is really useful for warm-ups for other exercises, and again, is more of a workout than you might think if you're used to dumbbells.

I haven't tried to do anything energetic with them, but a lot of the exercises feature a lot of graceful circular motions, so they might be well suited to something of the kind.

replies.title: Re: A Strange Awakening

I've taken two main things away from this: 1) old-timey calisthenics and gymnastics might be a good place to look for building a similar program, 2) Hatha Yoga might not be as "foreign" to Western occultism as it at first appears.

replies.title: Re: A Strange Awakening
replies.content: I learned more than a decade after I got those exercises from an old manual of the uniformed branch of the Odd Fellows -- yes, that's where a lot of that material came from -- that those exercises are called the Daily Dozen, they were invented by a man named Walter Camp, and they were very popular between the two world wars. You can find plenty of sources on them online these days, I'm delighted to say -- and they're very solid basic exercises, for what it's worth, well suited to daily practice.

replies.title: Re: A Strange Awakening
replies.content:
When B. K. S. Iyenghar first came to England and began to teach yoga, his first textbook ("Light on Yoga," 1966) was seen through the press by Gerald Yorke, who also wrote a preface to it. A lot of the book's organization and its presentation to its English audience seems to owe as much or more to Yorke than to Iyengar himself.

Moreover, Gerald Yorke himself had been a disciple of Aleister Crowley, whose very early work on yoga ("Book 4, Part 1," 1912) had made yoga a subject of great interest to occultists in the English-speaking world. So Iyengar Yoga in its early years indirectly owes a considerable something to Crowley and his work on yoga.

I haven't read Mark Singleton's book yet; does he mention the Crowley-York-Iyengar connection?

replies.title: Re: A Strange Awakening
replies.content:
Thank you for that information.....interesting! No, Singleton doesn’t get into that. He mentions the influence of the theosophists of course but doesn’t delve too deeply into western occultists’ interest in yoga......just some tantalizing tidbits which explored further would make a great book on it’s own.

His focus mainly is on the flashy, strongman type exercise regimes which eventually lead to modern bodybuilding and Bikram-style, high drama and highly competitive yoga.

SundaraYogaShala

1. I saw a documentary from a news organization infamous for its shock reporting on modern-day witchcraft in a certain Eastern European country. These are folk magic practitioners working in a matriarchal lineage, not the urbanite variety of pseudo-witches. Apparently their services are quite in demand these days and they enjoy a large clientele. One of their top offerings includes hostile magic (cursing, etc.), and they seem quite willing to perform it with the justification that it’s their job to do so. a) Do folk magic practitioners accrue negative karma from hostile magic in the same way as ceremonial magicians? b) I assume that traditional banishing spells such as the LBRP also protect against folk magic?

2. Immediately after I complete a session of magical practice (usually consisting of banishing + invocation), I experience a peculiar kind of headspace. I’m easily startled and sensitive to things in my surroundings, and I look at everything with childlike eyes, as if for the first time. In psychological language, it feels like my mind has been opened up and the grip of the ego has been temporarily loosened. It takes me a good amount of time to go back to being fully normal and grounded, and this effect seems to have increased after a couple of years of practice. The more intense my visualizations and state of trance during practice, the stronger the effect also. Could you comment on this experience? Am I going through something normal?

1b) That's certainly been my experience and that of others.

2) That's a known phenomenon. Try eating more red meat -- that helps ground you in your physical body, which is after all made of meat. Magic should not make you a space cadet...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'd like to offer my two cents on your second question. I have had similar experiences after a couple of days camping out at small to medium sized pagan festivals held in the country, when I've been spending most of my time alone communing with nature, hanging out with people I know and trust, sitting around a campfire singing, participating in well designed group rituals, etc. What's going on with me after a while is that I've let my usual guards down, and like you, am more open to the flow of interaction with the material and social environment in which I'm immersed. This is both mentally (in the narrow sense) and physically opening up. It can be compared to swimming in the ocean. It's something I actively seek and enjoy when conditions are right. For me, necessary conditions are a setting in nature and being with a group of people who have some degree of spiritual awareness, have decent manners and aren't trying to exploit each other.

After a few experiences of this kind, I figured out that a major part of this opening up experience is that, without making a conscious choice, I've allowed my aura to spread out much farther into the physical space around me than I normally do. It's the opposite of what people do in a crowded elevator: they can't avoid being in each other's personal space, so they hug their auras tight to avoid sharing energy with strangers.

An aura is like a cat's whiskers; it extends one's senses. I think the natural state of young children and babies is to have extended auras. When the outside world is threatening or hostile, one learns to pull the aura in closer to the skin. This becomes a habit, especially in cities, or when working a job where it isn't safe to be one's natural self, or when living with someone one doesn't get along with.

My advice to you is that when you are coming out of trance, take note of how far your aura is extended. For me, this is a feeling in the body, but some people can see their own auras. Draw the edges of it back closer to your body--make it more concentrated and less diffuse--until you feel comfortable and safe with where your boundaries are. If your practice includes putting up a shield or a protective sphere, take note of its condition and renew it as necessary.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This kind of thing happens almost always when you take up systematic spiritual practice, because you become less vulnerable to groupthink and to the collective trance that passes for consciousness in every human society. Manly P. Hall used to refer to the spiritual path as "the Way of the Lonely Ones" for exactly this reason. Yes, it's painful and confusing; yes, putting more time into your spiritual work is a good way to deal with that -- and you may also find, as many others have, that making contact with other people who share your interest in occultism and spiritual practice is a good way to find new friends.

So far as I know, routine banishing rituals weren't used by Medieval European magicians. (If you called Somthing up, you would want to send it down again once you had finished with it, of course, and there were rituals for t\hat--but that's quite another thing.)

What was done routinely were called "exorcisms," which were not at all the sort of thing you saw in the movie "The Exorcist," but very simple rituals made for anyone who could read Latin to use. During the later 1400s and early 1500s these routine exorcisms were published in very small booklets by the tens of thousands for anyone to buy and use if they felt troubled by a "malign spirit" or a "demon." Their titles were along the lines of Conjurationes daemonum or Conjuratio malignorum spirituum.

Here's an example available as a PDF onbline:

https://gallica.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/bpt6k60810q

And here's another:

https://books.google.com/books?id=CLbRzKXRZSMC&printsec=frontcover#v=onepage&q&f=false

I'm pretty sure any good search engine could turn up a few more.

Another Medieval method involved reading aloud either the first few verses of the Gospel accdording to John or the whole of Psalm 91 ("He that dwelleth in the secret place of the Most High,. etc.")

This is the Psalm that contains the well-known verses, "Thou shalt not be afraid for the terror by night; nor for the arrow that flieth by day; Nor for the pestilence that walketh in darkness; nor for the destruction that wasteth at noonday." Here is the source of the phrase "things that go bump in the night" (from another translation).

What we really need is more academic Medievalists who have not just studied Western Medieval texts of magic, but actually have practiced real magic in their own life, or at least experimented with it. I have a few--a very few--academic colleagues who I suspect may fit that description, but so far as I know, I'm the only academic out there who will admit to it quite openly.

(But then the field of Western Medieval Studies tends to attract committed and devout Christians--more often than not, Roman Catholics--for whom the practice of any magic is interdicted. And academia tends to attract materialists and staunch believers in the march of inevitable progress toward a brighter future. Ugh!)

As for myself, like our host I care little for what others may think of me, although prudence dictates a certain amount of 'flying under the radar." And I wasn't raised as a monotheist, but by a family with long generations of "counter-religious" and esoteric interests before me. ("Counter-religious" is to be understood by analogy with the modern term "counter-cultural." That older term was coined by Charles G. Leland back in 1899.)

comments.title: Theurgy
comments.content:
Thank you to JMG & Robert Mathiesen for your answers last week!

1. I've read some online sources on theurgy, and am wondering whether this is the genre/larger category that DMH-DA falls into, rather than something that would be added to this curriculum? When I invoke Hu, air, and "its, gods, spirits, and powers," that sounds theurgical to me.

replies.title: Re: Theurgy
replies.content:
1) The DMH-DA tradition, like most Western systems of high magic these days, is a mix of theurgy and thaumaturgy. It certainly doesn't need to be added in -- it's there.

2) I don't know of anyone who's written on the theurgy of the Druid Revival tradition at all! Iamblichus is probably your best bet, and Thomas Taylor's book on the Mysteries is also worth close reading.

3) I'm delighted to hear this -- and "The Valley of the Black Pig" is one of my favorite Yeats poems! It reliably gets the hair on the back of my neck to stand up. I'd encourage you to work with theurgic practice and see what you can develop.

comments.content:
Greetings Sir and Druid,

I was recently reading on the topic of paleoclimatology, and I noticed that a series of climate events (8.6k event was what perked my interest) roughly line up with change overs of astrological ages. Wikipedia for its faults has a century by century overview, and the cusps (autocorrect wants to change it to chaos) between eras where from legend was transmitted to much later has visible marks of the change in astrological eras. The two older eras that we have written documentation of, the Ariean, and Taurean, have aspects highlighting their direct opposite sign, as Shadows of the era signs. The beginning of the Taurean era has legends of the Scorpion king, the Ariean era has, in the rashness of Aries, the development of Libra’s balancing justice, and the Venerian sensuality pervading throughout.

Going back, further and further, we eventually get to the Leonian era ~12,000 years before he present. What is interesting is that the change over to the Leonian era is approximately marked by the Younger Dryas, which started at a local nadir of global temperatures, and first slowly and finally rapidly rose to the current Holocene optimum.

The immediately previous period (Virgonian?), the Bølling–Allerød warming, was marked by a steep decline of temperatures from an abrupt rising from the period (Libran?) before it dubbed the Oldest Dryas. It was as if there was a blip of a civilization that suddenly rose, peaked, and crashed after exhausting some non renewable resource in less than a millennium.

I’m timing the astrological era lengths by 2000 years and change, and the timing of the ice core data is also a bit fudgy. It is none the less fascinating to see astrological and geological cycles lineup.

The Bølling–Allerød warming period is instructive as its sudden rise and fall reminds me of a certain civilization of lore, and uncomfortably of own. This especially applies as we are entering the polar opposite of the Leonian-Aquarian pairing.

Has anyone written about the mutually receptive polarity of eras, the late Piscean era and its Virgoian shadow, the Ariean era and its Libran shadow, the Taurean era and its Scorpionic shadow, etc.?

But if you are interested in a different way of looking at earth's cycles and how they relate to movements of other bodies in the heavens - not only in our solar system, but in our galaxy - this Miles Mathis paper, together with his related work discussing shorter cycles influenced by planetary alignments, such as sunspot cycles etc, may interest you.

Granted, he is somewhat wary of his ideas being mistaken for astrology, but on the other hand, his way of approaching physics - essentially by restoring rigour to each and every equation by assigning every variable to a specific physical quality - is refreshingly unusual.

comments.content: Last Magic Monday Lady Cutekitten replied to a comment I made about reflecting energy back using agrimony. I had already turned off my computer for the evening so this is in answer to Lady Cutekitten and anyone else who is curious about agrimony. Agrimony is not poisonous or dangerous per se, and can be purchased at an apothecary, herb shop or online. It can also be grown fairly easily. While I have found it reflects energy back to its source (that means the users as well), I don't think it would repel an animal. For that, I recommend urine sprinkled around the edge of a property or compost pile or whatever you want protected. Did I just hear a loud eww, gross! Oh well, animals mark their territory and so can people;-) If you are unable to pee directly around the object/yard because of privacy concerns, then pee in a bucket, water it down a bit and put it in a watering can or jar, etc and sprinkle away. My husband suggests that you throw it up in the trees and bushes as well as animals are discouraged by something that can apparently pee 10 feet up a tree :-) It won't stink up the place unless you are a heavy drinker or drug user, but the animal/s will notice and skirt the area. Replenish as necessary after rain. We live in the woods with chickens, rabbits and gardens we want to protect and although we hear and see evidence of the coyotes, racoons, bears, skunks, etc., they rarely bother us.

replies.title: Re: Baphomet and friends
replies.content:
If I understand you correctly, you're asking about the analysis of the name Baphomet that Hugh Schoenfeld made public a while back. He pointed out that if you take the name of Baphomet in Hebrew and put it through temurah -- the Cabalistic process of replacing one letter with another -- using the Atbash formula, the most common form of temurah, you get the name of Sophia. That's something I've encountered in one of the Templar traditions in which I've been initiated, too, though I encountered that many years after Schoenfeld wrote about it and it's possible that the Templars borrowed it from him!

As for the Shekinah, you'd probably have to talk to some of my Jewish readers.

replies.title: Re: Baphomet and friends
replies.content: If I may: in Jewish lore, the Shekhinah or "Dwelling" is present whenever there's a minyan or at least 10 Jews present to worship.  I've attended Jewish services which met that criteria, and she was certainly there.  If you want to experience her and haven't already, I would find some time to attend a well attended Shabbat service at a synagogue that's friendly to visitors.  Then attend a service with a turnout of less than ten Halachic Jews.  The difference between the first service and the second is, according to the tradition as I understand it, the Shekhinah herself.

comments.title: Astrological magic vs geomantic magic
comments.content:
Good morning Mr. Greer!

This might not be a question anyone currently alive can answer, but here goes. You've compared astrology to the solar current as geomancy to the telluric current, but there is far more lore/literature/etc on astrological magic than on geomantic magic: is this because astrological magic is largely time-based and does not require travel as perhaps location-based telluric magic might? Or is it just a historical accident?

Best,
Andrew

replies.title: Re: Astrological magic vs geomantic magic
replies.content: It's a prestige thing. Astrology was much more prestigious than geomancy, since in the pre-computer period you had to know math to be able to use it. Geomancy was more popular among the lower classes because you can do it if you can tell the difference between odd and even numbers. Since writing books was mostly a habit of the well-to-do until fairly recently, in historical terms, astrology got all the press.

replies.title: Re: Astrological magic vs geomantic magic
replies.content: I'm mildly amused at just how often the answer to historical questions on nearly every topic can be summed up as "snobbery"...

replies.title: Re: Astrological magic vs geomantic magic
replies.content: Human beings are social primates, and like all other social primates, we define our world by social hierarchies and our relationship to them. So, yes, snobbery is one of the four primal forces that maintain the human social universe. (I'll leave it to you and my other readers to note the other three...)

comments.title: How to Not be Thinking Not For Yourself...
comments.content:
Hi JMG!

I've been working through the Order of the Octagon material and getting some great results. Sometimes to an almost Jedi degree... I was at the dentist last week nervously trying to figure out credit on emergency work, and tried shifting my consciousness to look at the ("mundane, mean") receptionist as a miraculous creation of God uniquely and constantly loved by them, and the same for me (with my perpetual "spaced out & arrogant" mien). All the sudden she figured out how to fix things from her end, get me a good deal, called me an endearment and started whistling happily.

It's remarkable to how much results could come from "just journaling". I'm working on the Law of Happiness now and find myself at random points in the day noticing myself being my old sour self and thinking "I consciously choose to be happy, I don't give a rat's bottom if I'm on the bus or a customer on the phone is hosing on me etc, I don't have @#$ time in my life not to be happy"

It is just remarkable how transformational this is. I realize that the way I've thought about spirituality all my life, based on childhood religious & family experiences, is sort of upside down, based on beating up on myself & others & trying to return to a childlike state of naivete.

Last week you recommended the Order of Essenes work to me, which I started on.

I'm the one who had the super upsetting ayahuasca experience. I'm continuing on the realization that my big "hidden weakness" is not really that stuff so much as my obsessive embarrassment, but even more so just a very dependent or other directed mentality. For example continually having crushes on bosses & therapists, having friends that I obsessively regurgitate every upsetting life event to, generally having my mind getting caught up in the drama of what I will say to whom, for no particular reason.

When I try to make decisions for myself, I have a lot of trouble knowing what I want and making it stick. Especially when money & stress make it feel I don't have choices... half the answer seems to be making plans and half of it to be changing my vibe. I'm thinking of just walling off others for a few weeks, but... I feel I've been there before, and it's not good, because I'm not really "on retreat", I'm scrapping for a living, and people from that will just grab my mind. And actually somehow I'm both codependent and also unreasonably wall others out, I never let any of these connections etc develop emotionally because I'm always suspicious of them as wrong.

I wonder what you think. Other than just continuing to do the work. The best I can come up with is just do the periodic survey of my life and try to say as best I can where I want to go with life & relationships, and stop myself whenever my brain goes back into drama land.

replies.title: Re: How to Not be Thinking Not For Yourself...
replies.content: Just keep on doing the work. Most people are other-directed rather than self-directed, and when they start waking up, they have to go through the same challenging process you're facing right now. Take it a step at a time, and watch the benefits pile up...

replies.title: Re: How to Not be Thinking Not For Yourself...
replies.content: Thanks Archdruid. Sometimes I feel I'm a special coal-black snowflake, messed up unlike any other and stuck forever. That makes me feel more hopeful.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Several years back, before I got into anything particularly esoteric, I noticed that I was drinking waaay too much coffee, and I cut way back. Then a friend of mine at work mentioned he switched to entirely decaffeinated coffee - you get (most of) the coffee taste and you can drink a lot without much ill effect. I followed his example for a few years, but I recently started earlier morning work, so I've gone back to one cup of real coffee in the morning, then decaf or tea after that.

A few possibly helpful tips from my experience:
1. If you go with decaf, even if you don't normally like flavoring, consider it. Decaf just plain doesn't taste as good as the real thing, and my regional grocer has a "San Antonio Blend" with some very subtle vanilla, cinnamon, and cocoa flavor that's pretty nice
2. If you keep having real coffee, as some others have said, try to avoid it for at least an hour after waking and after early-mid afternoon (I find 2:00 pm to be a good cutoff time to make sure it doesn't subtly affect my sleep).
3. Turns out some folks metabolize caffeine more slowly than others. If you feel a strong rush shortly after drinking coffee, you likely metabolize it fast - stronger effects, but they don't last as long. If you don't have as strong noticeable effects, you might metabolize slowly, which means you get a weaker effect for longer. I'm the latter, and it's because of this that I spent years thinking I had sleep-onset insomnia. I'd drink caffeine into the later afternoon or early evening, wouldn't feel like it was affecting me by bedtime, but then I wouldn't be able to sleep. If you have a milder initial reaction to caffeine, the early afternoon cutoff is even more important
4. If you don't want to mess with decaf (can't blame you), black chai tea has a nicely "strong" flavor thanks to the spices, and has more caffeine than green tea, but less than breakfast tea or coffee, which I find to be a pleasant balance between mild and stimulating. It also seems to go just fine with ritual and meditation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I do discuss it in a later essay, but I can also summarize it here. The difficulty is in way the initiation rituals interface with the Tree of Life. If you do a powerful ritual of initiation awakening the influence of a Sphere you aren't yet able to reach by other means, the energies of the Sphere come through in their unbalanced form, and you end up in a mess. The most common problem ritual was the Adeptus Minor, which is full of solar influences and so produced a bumper crop of over-the-top ego-driven jerks, but there are also embarrassing examples further down the ladder of degrees. That's why so many post-GD groups dropped the GD scheme of initiation while keeping the rest of the system -- Dion Fortune's group, for example, had three degrees of initiation unlinked with the Tree of Life -- and avoided that problem.

replies.title: Re: Haliverse Chronolgy
replies.content:
It's entirely off topic, but I'm willing to bend things where my fiction is concerned. ;-)

The Shoggoth Concerto begins the autumn before Dreamlands and ends about two weeks after Miriam Akeley returns from the Dreamlands. I recommend reading it after Dreamlands, so you catch some of the hints in the last scene, where Brecken performs her concerto for the first time.

A Voyage to Hyperborea takes place the year after Dreamlands, and The Nyogtha Variations takes place three years later, ending two months before the beginning of Providence.

The Seal of Yueh Lao takes place two years before Arkham.

So if you want to read the whole sequence in order, here you go:

WoH: Innsmouth
WoH: Kingsport
WoH: Chorazin
WoH: Dreamlands
The Shoggoth Concerto
A Voyage to Hyperborea
The Nyogtha Variations
WoH: Providence
WoH: Red Hook
The Seal of Yueh Lao
WoH: Arkham

comments.content:
There’s a lot of talk these strange days about transhumanism. Some are claiming that behind all the smoke and mirrors that is the Covid fiasco, what the jabs are really about is injecting people with graphene, nanoparticles, and I don’t know what else, or pushing nano-structures deep into the nasal cavity at the base of the brain using a swab as part of a test for Covid (see for instance the section at the end of this article: https://www.theautomaticearth.com/2021/09/spartacus/ ).

The point of doing this apparently, is to be able to control people’s thoughts, to operate them almost like marionettes; people could be made to dance on hot coals and smile while doing it, for example. It seems that transhumanists have bought the lie that consciousness is an epiphenomen of thinking. Regardless, those being remotely operated would be subject to the worst sort of slavery imaginable with no chance of escape.

Whatever the truth of the matter is, it got me to thinking about meditation, willpower, and magic. It also got me thinking about Frank Herbert’s epic novel, Dune.

When meditating, ideas that come up can oftentimes be traced back to previous thoughts. There are also thoughts that seem to come out of nowhere, and sometimes they seem to have a different kind of ‘flavor’ to them, somehow foreign to us. After a while we start to recognize these thoughts more easily as not being our own, that they come from somewhere or something else. This can be a useful ability if one is communicating with the gods, but it’s also useful when trying to maintain focus as extraneous thoughts can more easily be dismissed.

My thinking took me to the evolution of the human mind and I started to think that this horrible vision of the transhumanists controlling people’s minds directly could conversely provide the required pressure, the thrust block, for there to be a real evolutionary advantage to having advanced mental ability, being able to shut down foreign thoughts and impulses in order to deny the transhumanists their fun and a real way to escape tyranny and slavery.

Forced to develop this way, people’s mental abilities could expand enormously, acquiring prodigious abilities like those of the mentats in Dune. Abilities such as telepathy might become commonplace. Magic, too - the last refuge of the dispossessed - could reach new levels of sophistication and ultimately be used to overthrow the oppressors, relying as they do on their cheap gimmickry compared to real mind power.

I wonder what you think of all this, could the appalling oppression that some say is about to be imposed on us turn out to be a blessing in disguise?

Thank you Lady Cute Kitten for your gentle reminder to present ourselves attractively and appropriately, using good manners at all times. Seemingly little things like this are really pretty big things in making our day to day lives easier and more positive in general. 😺

Last week a couple of the gentlemen were talking about combining workouts with devotion to their chosen dieties. That is a wonderful idea to take Thor along to the gym! and this idea can be used many ways. For a couple of years I have been doing a practice with a friend called Calligraphy Yoga.....it’s a newish sort of cross between tai chi and yoga. Personally I don’t really think it’s either 😉 however it has graceful, continuous flowing movements that are helping me with some wrist problems, and it is very interesting when focused on after some careful study of the methods of Genevieve Stebbins (balance, gracefulness and correct breathing). After practicing for awhile I sort of spontaneously started assigning each “form” to a god or goddess in my chosen pantheon and a little group of four “forms” in the middle I assign to each of the four elements. Now, during our little warmup I dedicate the practice to my dieties. At one point we stand with arms lifted like in a hug, for a minimum of 5 minutes and that is a wonderful opportunity for prayer. I look forward to this practice three times a week at dawn and feel very relaxed and uplifted afterwards. With some creativity any form of exercise can be a devotion and you will get so much more out of it!

Also last week some parents of young children were talking about issues with nightmares. When my daughters were little and problems started to crop up like nightmares and later separation anxiety, I got them each a small carved wooden Ganesha. We have a bigger Ganesha where we burn incense and talk to him. The girls kept their little Ganeshas next to their beds for protection from various monsters (because it is known that Ganesha has zero patience for monsters disturbing the beauty sleep of little girls and his jolly self!) and that helped immensely. Later they took little Ganesh in their backpacks to school so that whenever they felt anxious or sad they could reach in and hold Ganesha for a minute and feel strong. Of course something like this can’t be forced on kids! Mine were already used to my devotion to Ganesha and he is very kid friendly. What’s not to like about a portly, elephant-headed god who loves offerings of cookies 😀. Hope that gives some parents some ideas. Life can be tough on little kids!

Thank you so much for this forum, fine teacher, and may all of us have a bit of joy on this day.

Translucent Concupiscent Cactus ~~~ SundaraYogaShala

comments.title: Dogma
comments.content: Good evening! A topic of conversation that has come up recently within discussions of AODA is the topic of dogma. As the AODA presents itself to be dogma free, some folks have started questioning whether the required practice of the SOP is dogmatic.  Some folks seem to think so and others not so much. Do you have any thoughts on this?

replies.title: Re: Dogma
replies.content: The people who make that argument are misusing the term "dogma." A dogma is something you have to believe. The SoP is something you have to practice, which is not the same thing at all. You don't have to have any particular opinion about the SoP; you just have to do it, and learn from it. (And if you don't want to do it and learn from it, why, there are plenty of other Druid orders out there which you can join instead.)

replies.title: Re: Dogma
replies.content: Thank you for your thoughts! That was my understanding of the SoP and dogma as well.

replies.title: Re: Dogma
replies.content:
If I may, dogma is what you must believe: it is a form of orthodoxy, coming from two Greek roots: ortho, meaning "correct", doxa, meaning "belief. This word is well known, in large part due to the way in which Christianity, especially the western forms, are heavy on their orthodoxy. Much more so, in fact, than most religions in the world.

What the SOP in the AODA is is not orthodoxy, but a related concept: orthopraxy (which is rare enough that many spell checkers do not recognize it). Orthopraxy comes from two Greek roots: ortho, "correct" again, and praxis, "action". This is what the role of the SOP is within the AODA: it's an orthopraxy.

I hope that this distinction helps.

replies.title: Re: Dogma
replies.content: That does make more sense. It seems that a large amount or just about all magical orders and Pagan groups include orthopraxy.

replies.title: Re: Dogma
replies.content: At the same time, I struggle to label it as a full-on orthopraxy. While the practice itself is a requirement of members, outside of providing a framework there is neither a specific way in which it must be done nor is there a specific orthodoxy behind the orthopraxy. The ritual itself is very flexible and can be modified for different uses. It's an interesting one in the least.
